techniques.name: Ultrasonic sonar vital sign extraction (inaudible Doppler physiological sensing)
techniques.description: A phone or earbud speaker emits an inaudible continuous-wave ultrasonic tone (18-22 kHz, within transducer bandwidth but above human hearing threshold). The built-in microphone captures the reflected signal. Chest wall motion from breathing (amplitude: ~5mm) and heartbeat (amplitude: ~0.1mm) create Doppler shifts in the reflected ultrasound that are demodulable with standard DSP. The technique is: (1) covert — the ultrasonic tone is inaudible to the target, (2) contactless — works from across a room (demonstrated up to 0.5m for heart rate, several meters for respiration), (3) requires NO hardware modification — stock smartphone speakers and microphones are sufficient, and (4) can be deployed as a background process in any app with microphone permission. Google's Nest Hub Sleep Sensing and academic research (UltraSense, Nandakumar et al.) have demonstrated production-quality vital sign extraction via this method. Attack scenario: any app with mic access silently emits ultrasound and extracts heart rate, breathing rate, and movement patterns. Combined with QIF-T0079 (ear canal fingerprinting), the attacker gets identity + vitals from the same acoustic pipeline.

techniques.name: Haptic motor body sonar (taptic engine repurposing for tissue impedance profiling)
techniques.description: A phone's haptic actuator (e.g., Apple Taptic Engine, linear resonant actuator) is driven at known frequencies (100 Hz - 5 kHz sweep) while in contact with the body. The built-in accelerometer and/or microphone measures the tissue response: acoustic impedance varies by tissue type (bone, muscle, fat, fluid), depth, and composition. This is a simplified form of acoustic impedance spectroscopy or elastography using consumer hardware. The technique requires: (1) physical contact between device and body (phone against skin), (2) firmware-level or jailbreak access to drive the haptic motor at arbitrary frequencies (consumer APIs limit haptic patterns), and (3) raw accelerometer access at high sample rates. Potential extractions: body composition estimation, subcutaneous fluid detection (edema), bone density approximation, tissue stiffness changes. This is significantly lower resolution than clinical ultrasound elastography but could distinguish gross tissue categories. The attack surface is narrow (requires physical contact + firmware access), but wearable devices (Apple Watch, fitness bands) that maintain constant skin contact and contain both haptic motors and accelerometers present an always-on version of this attack.

techniques.name: IR vascular mapping via Face ID system (NIR hemoglobin absorption imaging)
techniques.description: Apple's Face ID TrueDepth system projects 30,000 infrared dots at 940nm onto the user's face and reads the reflection pattern with an IR camera. At 940nm, photons penetrate skin to a depth of 2-5mm — well into the dermal vascular layer. Oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO2) and deoxygenated hemoglobin (Hb) have different absorption coefficients at 940nm, meaning the reflected dot pattern encodes subsurface vascular topology: arterial vs venous vessels, vessel diameter, branching patterns, and oxygenation gradients. This vascular map is a permanent biometric (unlike facial features, which change with age/surgery/expression) and is unique per individual (even identical twins have different vascular topology). In the attack scenario, a jailbroken iPhone or compromised Face ID firmware extracts raw IR reflection data (normally processed in the Secure Enclave and discarded) during routine phone unlock. The target never knows their vascular biometric has been captured. Every phone unlock becomes a silent biometric scan. This is analogous to fingerprinting (unreplaceable biometric) but captured at range and without the target's awareness. Combined with QIF-T0078 (pulse waveform), the same IR system yields both vascular structure and cardiac dynamics.

cvss.gap_summary: Permanent vascular biometric extraction partially captured by CVSS; unreplaceable biometric loss (like neural biometric) not fully expressible

techniques.name: LiDAR remote pulse detection (laser Doppler vibrometry for cardiac waveform extraction)
techniques.description: The iPhone LiDAR scanner uses a VCSEL (vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser) array emitting pulsed 940nm infrared laser light and a SPAD (single-photon avalanche diode) detector array measuring time-of-flight. In normal operation, it measures depth for AR applications. However, the same hardware can function as a simplified laser Doppler vibrometer (LDV): when the laser beam reflects off skin, the pulse wave from cardiac activity causes micro-vibrations (amplitude ~1-10 µm) on the skin surface. These vibrations create measurable Doppler shifts or time-of-flight variations in the reflected laser signal. Extracting the cardiac pulse waveform from LiDAR data requires raw access to the SPAD photodetector output (timing resolution in picoseconds) rather than the processed depth map. This requires either a jailbreak, hardware teardown, or compromised firmware. The extracted pulse waveform contains: heart rate, heart rate variability (HRV), pulse transit time (correlated with blood pressure), and potentially cardiac arrhythmia signatures. Unlike QIF-T0075 (ultrasonic sonar), this is optical and directional, requiring line-of-sight but working at greater precision for skin-surface vibrations. Range is limited to ~5m (LiDAR operational range) but could be extended with higher-power laser sources.

techniques.name: Ear canal acoustic fingerprinting (ANC echo profiling for covert identification)
techniques.description: Active noise cancellation (ANC) earbuds already contain the complete hardware for ear canal acoustic fingerprinting: an inward-facing (feedback) microphone that measures sound inside the ear canal, an outward-facing (feedforward) microphone, and a speaker that can emit probe tones. When the speaker emits a broadband chirp or swept sine, the inward-facing microphone captures the echo profile shaped by the ear canal's unique geometry: length (~25mm), diameter (~7mm), tympanic membrane compliance, and the specific curvature of the bends. This acoustic transfer function is a biometric — NEC demonstrated it for authentication with >99% accuracy. In the attack scenario, the ANC system's existing probe tones (used for adaptive fit detection and transparency mode calibration) are leveraged to silently fingerprint the wearer without their knowledge. The earbuds know WHO is wearing them at all times. Combined with audioplethysmography (PPG via in-ear speaker/mic measuring blood volume changes), the same hardware simultaneously provides identity + heart rate: a silent surveillance pipeline requiring zero hardware modification on ANC earbuds. Attack surface: firmware update, compromised ANC calibration routine, or malicious SDK in earbud companion app.

techniques.name: Gyroscope acoustic eavesdropping (MEMS speech capture via resonant frequency aliasing)
techniques.description: MEMS gyroscopes in smartphones sample at 100-200 Hz, with mechanical resonant frequencies in the audible speech range (100-8000 Hz). techniques.name: WiFi CSI passive body sensing (through-wall vital signs, 3D pose reconstruction, respiratory and gait biometric inference via dedicated or commodity WiFi hardware)
techniques.description: WiFi Channel State Information (CSI) captures the multipath propagation characteristics between WiFi transmitter and receiver. Human body movements — including breathing (chest wall motion ~5mm), heartbeat (body surface vibration ~0.1mm), and walking — modulate the WiFi signal propagation paths. Two attack profiles: (1) DEDICATED HARDWARE — modified firmware on CSI-capable APs enables through-wall sensing (2-3 standard walls), 3D pose reconstruction, and multi-person vital sign extraction at up to 8m range. (2) CONSUMER-GRADE — standard WiFi chipsets (Intel 5300, Atheros, ESP32) with CSI-enabled drivers extract respiratory rate (±1 bpm at 3m) and gait identity (93% single-person, 78% multi-person) without dedicated hardware. Respiratory modulation: phase shift Δφ = 4π×Δd/λ ≈ 0.4-1.2 radians at 5 GHz (chest displacement 4-12mm). Gait produces Doppler shifts f_d = 2v×cos(θ)/λ ≈ 40 Hz at walking speed. CSI matrix: H(f,t) ∈ C^(N_tx × N_rx × N_sub). CRITICAL REGULATORY GAP: Respiratory rate = PHI under HIPAA (45 CFR 160.103) when linked to individual. Gait biometric = special category data under GDPR Art. 9. No consent mechanism exists for incidental WiFi CSI health data collection — a router collecting CSI for 'network optimization' simultaneously collects respiratory data from everyone in range. This is passive radar using existing infrastructure.

cvss.gap_summary: Through-wall passive surveillance via WiFi; health privacy (HIPAA PHI), behavioral biometric (GDPR Art. 9), and regulatory consent gap dimensions not expressible in CVSS. Consumer-grade variant (PR:N/AT:N) has lower barrier to entry.

techniques.name: BLE physical-layer device fingerprinting (radio frequency imperfection tracking)
techniques.description: Every Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) transmitter has unique analog imperfections in its radio hardware: carrier frequency offset (CFO), I/Q imbalance, power amplifier nonlinearity, and phase noise characteristics. These imperfections are manufacturing artifacts that are stable, unique per device, and impossible to change via software — they are the RF equivalent of a fingerprint. Becker et al. (2022) demonstrated that BLE physical-layer fingerprinting can track devices even when using MAC address randomization (the privacy feature specifically designed to prevent tracking). This defeats Apple's and Google's BLE privacy protections. Attack scenario: passive BLE receivers at strategic locations (malls, airports, streets) fingerprint passing devices. The user's phone continuously advertises BLE (for AirDrop, Find My, COVID exposure notifications), and each advertisement carries the device's unchangeable RF fingerprint. This enables persistent location tracking despite all software-level privacy measures.

techniques.name: PPG pulse waveform biometric identification (cardiac signature fingerprinting via wearable optical sensor)
techniques.description: Photoplethysmography (PPG) sensors in smartwatches and fitness trackers measure blood volume changes through green LED light reflected from the wrist. The PPG waveform shape is unique per individual — determined by cardiac output, arterial stiffness, vessel geometry, and autonomic tone. Biswas et al. Unlike heart rate (a single number), the full PPG waveform is a rich biometric containing: pulse amplitude, dicrotic notch depth, systolic/diastolic ratio, pulse transit time, and waveform morphology. This biometric is continuously captured by any wearable with a heart rate sensor (Apple Watch, Fitbit, Galaxy Watch, Oura Ring). The user consents to heart rate monitoring, not to biometric identification from their cardiac waveform. Combined with T0088 (gait) and T0079 (ear canal), the attacker has three independent biometric channels from consumer devices.

techniques.name: Neural steganographic encoding (inaudible audio watermarking for covert neural command/biofingerprint channel)
techniques.description: Embedding data in audio signals below the human hearing threshold (>18 kHz ultrasonic or <20 Hz infrasonic) or within psychoacoustic masking bands of audible content. The encoded signal is imperceptible to the listener but decodable by a receiver with knowledge of the encoding scheme. In the adversarial case, the hidden channel carries covert commands, tracking identifiers, or subliminal cues targeting auditory processing pathways (e.g., triggering pre-conditioned responses or influencing decision-making). In the protective case, the same physics enables steganographic embedding of neural biofingerprints for authentication — the signal evokes a measurable auditory evoked potential (AEP) unique to the individual's cochlear geometry and auditory cortex response, functioning as a biometric watermark. Initial enrollment/profiling phase required to capture individual's unique AEP response. Practical constraints include target device's ability to accurately reproduce encoded frequencies and effective transmission range (1-10m). Psychoacoustic masking threshold follows: T_mask(f) = L_masker - (sf × |f - f_masker|) where sf is the spreading factor (~25 dB/Bark on upper slope, ~10 dB/Bark on lower slope). Auditory steady-state response (ASSR) at 40 Hz provides the mechanism for neural biofingerprint verification. Proves TARA's cross-domain dual-use thesis: same physics operates as attack vector, defense mechanism, and therapeutic tool.

cvss.gap_summary: Covert channel partially captured by CVSS confidentiality; neural biofingerprint and subliminal cognitive dimensions not expressible

techniques.name: Multi-modal keystroke inference via acoustic-optical-RF fusion (password/input recovery without mic/camera permissions)
techniques.description: Fusion of three independent side channels from a single mobile device to reconstruct user input without requiring microphone, camera, or accessibility permissions. (1) Keystroke acoustic emanations: each key press produces a distinct acoustic signature (1-20 kHz broadband impulse, classifiable via MFCC + CNN at ~95% accuracy on laptops, lower but still viable on touchscreens). (2) Screen optical emission: display luminance changes of ~0.1-1% per character insertion detectable by ambient light sensor at <0.01 lux sensitivity. (3) WiFi CSI: finger movements modulate OFDM subcarrier phase — σ²_phase > threshold indicates keystroke events. Individual channel accuracy: 60-70% acoustic, 40-50% optical, 55-65% WiFi CSI. Fused via CRF/LSTM with temporal cross-correlation alignment: >95% accuracy with 30+ training samples per key. Critical insight: apps requesting speaker + WiFi permissions (trivially granted) achieve side-channel equivalent to camera + microphone (heavily restricted). Permission model does not reflect actual threat.

cvss.gap_summary: Multi-modal side-channel partially captured by CVSS confidentiality; behavioral biometric inference and permission model gap not expressible

techniques.name: Passive facial geometry estimation via display-as-illuminator inverse photometry (presence/orientation detection without camera)
techniques.description: The device display functions as a structured light source — each frame emits a known photon distribution. Reflected light from the user's face is captured by the ambient light sensor (ALS). The known spectral emission S(x,y,λ) and measured reflected irradiance E_sensor = ∫∫ ρ(x,y)cos(θ)S(x,y,λ)/r² dA constrain an inverse photometry problem. CRITICAL FEASIBILITY CAVEAT: A single ALS integrates the entire reflected light field into one scalar value, making 3D geometric reconstruction an ill-posed inverse problem. With current single-sensor hardware, achievable resolution is limited to basic presence detection, head orientation, and coarse proximity estimation — NOT high-fidelity facial geometry. Identity matching might be feasible only against a small template library with strong a priori constraints. Future multi-pixel ALS or multi-sensor arrays could significantly improve reconstruction fidelity. Despite geometric limitations, the same ALS reliably detects physiological signals: pulse-modulated skin reflectance for PPG heart rate (demonstrated in Samsung Galaxy phones) and skin color variations for SpO2 estimation.

cvss.gap_summary: Ambient light sensor side channel partially captured by CVSS; physiological data extraction dimension not expressible

techniques.name: SSVEP Frequency Hijack via Imperceptible Display Flicker
techniques.description: Exploit the SSVEP pathway by injecting imperceptible display flicker (above critical flicker fusion threshold, ~60Hz) that drives visual cortex responses without user awareness. Proven: Ming et al. 2023 demonstrated 60Hz flickers invisible to users produce classifiable SSVEP responses at 52.8 bits/min. Attack scenarios: (1) inject false BCI commands by matching SSVEP control frequencies, (2) jam BCI operation with broadband visual noise, (3) exfiltrate neural state via stimulus-response probing, (4) trigger photosensitive seizures at epileptogenic frequencies. Unlike T0040 (neurophishing via app-layer stimuli), this attack operates at the display hardware level and requires no BCI application cooperation. The display itself becomes the attack vector.

cvss.gap_summary: CVSS cannot express neural command injection via visual pathway; seizure induction risk has no CVSS dimension

sources: Ming et al. 2023 (J Neural Engineering, 60Hz imperceptible SSVEP, 52.8 bits/min)
Bian et al. 2022 (J Neural Engineering, SSVEP square wave attacks)
Upadhayay & Behzadan 2023 (IEEE SMC, sensory-channel BCI attacks)
Meng et al. 2024 (Future Gen Comp Sys, adversarial EEG filtering)

tara.mechanism: Display renders imperceptible flicker patterns at frequencies matching SSVEP response bands. Visual cortex phase-locks to stimulus below conscious awareness. BCI decoder interprets evoked response as user command or is jammed by broadband interference.

clinical.therapeutic_analog: High-frequency SSVEP-based BCI for locked-in patients
conditions: attention assessment via covert SSVEP monitoring

techniques.name: Neural spoofing
techniques.description: Forge neural identity to impersonate a legitimate BCI node or user. Analogous to IP/ARP spoofing. From Murcia neural cyberattack taxonomy (8 attacks: flooding, jamming, scanning, selective forwarding, spoofing, sybil, sinkhole, nonce). QIF-T0025-T0028 cover 4; this and T0105-T0107 cover the remaining 4.
sources: Lopez Bernal et al. 2023 (CACM, Eight Reasons to Prioritize BCI Cybersecurity)

techniques.name: Neural sybil
techniques.description: Create multiple fake neural node identities to overwhelm consensus or trust mechanisms in multi-electrode/multi-node BCI networks. Analogous to Sybil attacks in distributed systems. From Murcia neural cyberattack taxonomy.
sources: Lopez Bernal et al. 2023 (CACM, Eight Reasons to Prioritize BCI Cybersecurity)

techniques.name: Neural sinkhole
techniques.description: Attract and drop neural signals by advertising a compromised node as optimal routing point. Neural signals are diverted and silently discarded. Analogous to sinkhole attacks in sensor networks. From Murcia neural cyberattack taxonomy.
sources: Lopez Bernal et al. 2023 (CACM, Eight Reasons to Prioritize BCI Cybersecurity)

techniques.name: Neural nonce replay
techniques.description: Replay previously valid neural signal nonces to bypass freshness/replay protections. Exploits lack of temporal validation in neural signal authentication. Analogous to nonce replay in cryptographic protocols. From Murcia neural cyberattack taxonomy.
sources: Lopez Bernal et al. 2023 (CACM, Eight Reasons to Prioritize BCI Cybersecurity)

techniques.name: Neuromorphic mimicry attack (synaptic weight tampering)
techniques.description: Tamper with synaptic weights or inject poisoned sensory input into neuromorphic/SNN hardware (next-gen BCI processors). Evades traditional IDS by mimicking legitimate neural activity patterns. Input poisoning ~90% success, weight tampering ~83%. Traditional IDS detects only 12-15%.
sources: arXiv:2505.17094 (2025, Neuromorphic Mimicry Attacks)
arXiv:2601.16589 (2026, Emerging Threats in Neuromorphic Systems)

techniques.name: Data alignment exploitation
techniques.description: Target the data alignment step used by adversarial defenses (ABAT, A3E). If defenders use Euclidean alignment or reference matrices for cross-session EEG normalization, an attacker who can influence alignment reference data can bias the alignment transform, causing downstream misclassification. QIF-derived: inferred from defense architecture analysis.
sources: Derived from ABAT (Wu et al. 2024, IEEE TNSRE) and A3E (arXiv:2412.11390, 2024) defense papers

tactics.description: EM/RF attacks on neural tissue or BCI hardware via frequency-domain coupling. No traditional cybersecurity equivalent.

techniques.name: Neural steganographic encoding (inaudible audio watermarking for covert neural command/biofingerprint channel)
techniques.description: Embedding data in audio signals below the human hearing threshold (>18 kHz ultrasonic or <20 Hz infrasonic) or within psychoacoustic masking bands of audible content. The encoded signal is imperceptible to the listener but decodable by a receiver with knowledge of the encoding scheme. In the adversarial case, the hidden channel carries covert commands, tracking identifiers, or subliminal cues targeting auditory processing pathways (e.g., triggering pre-conditioned responses or influencing decision-making). In the protective case, the same physics enables steganographic embedding of neural biofingerprints for authentication — the signal evokes a measurable auditory evoked potential (AEP) unique to the individual's cochlear geometry and auditory cortex response, functioning as a biometric watermark. Initial enrollment/profiling phase required to capture individual's unique AEP response. Practical constraints include target device's ability to accurately reproduce encoded frequencies and effective transmission range (1-10m). Psychoacoustic masking threshold follows: T_mask(f) = L_masker - (sf × |f - f_masker|) where sf is the spreading factor (~25 dB/Bark on upper slope, ~10 dB/Bark on lower slope). Auditory steady-state response (ASSR) at 40 Hz provides the mechanism for neural biofingerprint verification. Proves TARA's cross-domain dual-use thesis: same physics operates as attack vector, defense mechanism, and therapeutic tool.

inventory.name: N1 Implant (Link)
inventory.fdaStatus: De novo classification granted Jan 2025; IDE clinical trial (PRIME) ongoing

channels.notes: 32 electrodes per thread x 64 threads. ASIC supports 3072 channels (12 x 256-ch chips) but current implant uses 1024.

samplingRate.notes: On-chip ADC at 19.3 kSPS per channel, 10-bit resolution.

power.notes: Approximately 6 mW total ASIC power including clock drivers.

dimensions.notes: Circular coin-shaped device implanted flush with skull.

wireless.notes: Custom BLE implementation for data streaming.

batteryLife.notes: Wirelessly rechargeable via inductive charger. Exact battery capacity not disclosed.

electrodeMaterial.value: Gold traces on polyimide threads; electrode tips treated with iridium oxide (IrOx) or PEDOT:PSS

snr.notes: Not explicitly published. 0.83 dB SNR loss at 100 kOhm impedance with 1 MOhm input impedance reported.

thermalBudget.value: < 40 mW system budget (FDA guideline for cortical implants)
thermalBudget.source: FDA BCI guidance 2021; Neuralink press materials
thermalBudget.notes: Thermal sensors monitor temperature during charging; auto-adjusts power transfer.

dataRate.notes: 1024 channels x 20 kHz x 10 bits = ~200 Mbps raw. On-chip compression reduces transmitted data.

regions.name: Prefrontal Cortex
regions.function: Executive function, decision-making, working memory, personality, social behavior, planning

response_latency_ms.measure: Visual stimulus to PFC neuronal onset
response_latency_ms.notes: Highly variable by task and stimulus type. Visual motion onset 40-60ms in frontal eye fields; decision-related activity 150-300ms.
citations: Sugase-Miyamoto et al. 2008 (PNAS): PFC population activity 100-200ms
Romanski & Hwang 2012 (PMC3618972): VLPFC vocalization response shortest latency

regions.name: Primary Motor Cortex
regions.function: Voluntary motor execution, somatotopic motor map (homunculus)

response_latency_ms.measure: Corticospinal conduction time to hand muscles
response_latency_ms.notes: Central motor conduction time (CMCT) measured via TMS: ~20ms to hand. Cortical motor neurons activate ~100-150ms before movement onset.
citations: Rossini et al. 2015: TMS guidelines, CMCT norms
Hallett 2007 (Neurology): TMS clinical neurophysiology

regions.name: Primary Visual Cortex
regions.function: Primary visual processing — edge detection, orientation, spatial frequency, color

response_latency_ms.measure: Visual stimulus to V1 ERP onset (C1 component)
response_latency_ms.notes: C1 component (N75): 56-100ms post-stimulus. Mean onset ~56ms (earliest reported). Intracranial: category decoding by 100ms.
citations: Clark et al. 1994 (Electroencephalogr Clin Neurophysiol): C1 mean onset 56ms
Di Russo et al. 2002 (PubMed 11797091): V1 source of C1 60-100ms
Liu et al. 2009 (J Neurosci): Object decoding from intracranial EEG at 100ms

regions.name: Primary Auditory Cortex
regions.function: Primary auditory processing — tonotopic frequency mapping, sound onset detection

response_latency_ms.measure: Auditory stimulus to cortical onset (Pa/high-gamma)
response_latency_ms.notes: Middle-latency response Pa: 25-40ms. High-gamma (70-150 Hz) cortical response ~40ms. Full cortical processing: Na 12-27ms, Pa 25-40ms.
citations: Liégeois-Chauvel et al. 1994 (J Neurosci): First-spike timing in auditory cortex
Nourski et al. 2022 (PMC9773383): Time-locked high-gamma responses at ~40ms
Picton et al. 2010 (PMC9443762): Middle-latency auditory response norms

regions.name: Broca's Area
regions.function: Speech production, syntactic processing, language planning

response_latency_ms.measure: Three sequential processing waves during linguistic tasks (human intracranial)
response_latency_ms.notes: Unique three-wave pattern: ~200ms (lexical), ~320ms (grammatical), ~450ms (phonological) — Flinker et al. 2015, intracranial EEG. High-gamma (70-150 Hz) during speech production and perception.
citations: Flinker et al. 2015 (PNAS): Three-wave pattern ~200/320/450ms in Broca's area
Sahin et al. 2009 (Science): Intracranial recordings lexical ~200ms, grammatical ~320ms, phonological ~450ms

regions.name: Wernicke's Area
regions.function: Language comprehension, semantic processing, speech reception

response_latency_ms.measure: Auditory speech to posterior STG high-gamma onset (human intracranial)
response_latency_ms.notes: High-gamma onset in posterior STG: ~50-100ms (intracranial, Mesgarani & Chang 2012). N400 semantic component peaks ~400ms. MMN at ~100-200ms for deviant phonemes.
citations: Mesgarani & Chang 2012 (Nature 485:233-236): STG speech response onset ~50ms
Kutas & Federmeier 2011 (Annu Rev Psychol): N400 review

regions.name: Premotor Cortex
regions.function: Motor planning, movement preparation, sensorimotor integration

response_latency_ms.measure: Visual cue to premotor neuron firing onset
response_latency_ms.notes: Premotor neurons fire 80-200ms before movement onset. Visually guided reach: ~120ms.
citations: Churchland et al. 2010 (Neuron): Preparatory activity in premotor cortex

regions.name: Supplementary Motor Area
regions.function: Motor sequence planning, bimanual coordination, internally generated movements

response_latency_ms.measure: Bereitschaftspotential (readiness potential) before movement
response_latency_ms.notes: Readiness potential begins ~1500ms before voluntary movement onset. SMA proper activates ~500ms before movement.
citations: Shibasaki & Hallett 2006 (Clin Neurophysiol): Bereitschaftspotential review

regions.name: Posterior Parietal Cortex
regions.function: Spatial awareness, sensorimotor integration, attention, reach planning

response_latency_ms.measure: Visual stimulus to LIP neuron onset (macaque single-unit); human intracranial ~60ms
response_latency_ms.notes: Mean latency 45.2ms in macaque area LIP (Bisley et al. 2004). Human posterior IPS: ~60ms (Hertz et al. 2018, intracranial). P1 ERP component over parietal cortex: ~100ms.
citations: Bisley et al. 2004 (J Neurosci 24(8):1833, DOI: 10.1523/JNEUROSCI.5007-03.2004): Mean latency 45.2ms
Hertz et al. 2018 (DOI: 10.1101/377887): Human intracranial ~60ms

regions.name: Primary Somatosensory Cortex
regions.function: Tactile sensation, proprioception, somatotopic sensory map (homunculus)

response_latency_ms.measure: Somatosensory evoked potential (SEP) N20 component
response_latency_ms.notes: N20 component at ~20ms post-median nerve stimulus. Earliest cortical SEP. P14 (subcortical) at ~14ms.
citations: Allison et al. 1991 (Cereb Cortex): Human cortical somatosensory evoked potentials
Cruccu et al. 2008 (Clin Neurophysiol): SEP guidelines

regions.name: Hippocampus
regions.function: Episodic memory formation, spatial navigation, memory consolidation

response_latency_ms.measure: Visual stimulus to hippocampal single-neuron response (human intracranial)
response_latency_ms.notes: Median onset 204ms for visually responsive neurons (Mormann et al. 2008, human intracranial). Hierarchical: parahippocampal cortex ~101ms, entorhinal cortex ~206ms, hippocampus ~204ms. Sharp-wave ripples (SWR): 80-200 Hz, ~50-100ms duration.
citations: Mormann, Kornblith, Quiroga et al. 2008 (J Neurosci 28(36):8865-8872, DOI: 10.1523/JNEUROSCI.1640-08.2008): Median onset 204ms
Buzsaki 2015 (Neuron): Sharp-wave ripples review

regions.name: Basolateral Amygdala
regions.function: Fear conditioning, emotional valence assignment, associative learning. Cortical-like architecture.

response_latency_ms.measure: Visual threat stimulus to amygdala response (intracranial EEG)
response_latency_ms.notes: Fastest documented: ~74ms via intracranial EEG (Mendez-Bertolo et al. 2016). NOT 12ms — no source exists for sub-20ms amygdala response. The 'fast path' bypasses thalamic gating but still requires ~74ms.
citations: Mendez-Bertolo et al. 2016 (Nature Neuroscience): Amygdala response to fear at 74ms via intracranial EEG

regions.name: Insular Cortex
regions.function: Interoception, pain processing, emotional awareness, taste, autonomic regulation

response_latency_ms.measure: Auditory stimulus to insular HFA onset (human intracranial EEG)
response_latency_ms.notes: Intracranial EEG: 200-350ms for auditory stimuli. Visual P300-like component in anterior insula: 250-338ms. Substantially slower than primary sensory cortices, consistent with higher-order integrative role.
citations: Demarchi et al. 2019 (PMID: 31629197): Auditory deviance response in insula
Ibos et al. 2019 (DOI: 10.1007/s00429-019-01892-y): Visual oddball insular response

regions.name: Anterior Cingulate Cortex
regions.function: Conflict monitoring, error detection, pain processing, motivation, autonomic regulation

response_latency_ms.measure: ERN (Error-Related Negativity) onset
response_latency_ms.notes: Error-related negativity (ERN) peaks ~80-150ms post-error. Conflict monitoring (N2): ~200-350ms. Pain processing: ~150-250ms.
citations: Gehring et al. 2012 (Oxford Handbook): ERN and ACC

regions.name: Cingulate Gyrus (Posterior)
regions.function: Default mode network hub, self-referential processing, memory retrieval, spatial orientation

response_latency_ms.measure: Task-related deactivation/activation
response_latency_ms.notes: Part of default mode network. Deactivation during task onset: ~200-300ms. Memory-related activation: variable.
citations: Raichle 2015 (Neuron): Default mode network review

regions.name: Striatum (Caudate + Putamen)
regions.function: Motor selection, habit learning, reward prediction, action initiation

response_latency_ms.measure: Reward-related or movement-related striatal firing
response_latency_ms.notes: Reward prediction error: ~100ms post-outcome. Movement-related: ~60-200ms before movement in putamen. Caudate: 100-300ms for cognitive tasks.
citations: Schultz 2006 (Annu Rev Psychol): Reward signals in basal ganglia

regions.name: Globus Pallidus Internus
regions.function: Primary output nucleus of basal ganglia (inhibitory). Major DBS target for dystonia.

response_latency_ms.measure: Striatal input to GPi output latency
response_latency_ms.notes: Direct pathway: striatum → GPi ~10-15ms. GPi tonic firing rate: 60-80 Hz. Pause latency during movement: ~15ms.
citations: Kita & Kita 2011 (J Neurosci): Basal ganglia circuit timing

regions.name: Globus Pallidus Externus
regions.function: Indirect pathway relay, tonic inhibition of STN, basal ganglia modulation

response_latency_ms.measure: Striatal input to GPe relay
response_latency_ms.notes: Similar timing to GPi. Tonic firing rate: 50-70 Hz. Indirect pathway involves additional STN relay.
citations: Kita & Kita 2011 (J Neurosci): Basal ganglia circuit timing

regions.name: Subthalamic Nucleus
regions.function: Hyperdirect pathway hub, motor urgency signal, stop-signal processing. Primary DBS target for Parkinson's.

response_latency_ms.measure: Cortical stimulation to STN response (antidromic/orthodromic)
response_latency_ms.notes: Short-latency (antidromic via hyperdirect pathway): 5-10ms. Medium-latency (antidromic cortical): 2-15ms. Long-latency (orthodromic polysynaptic): 20-70ms. Beta oscillations (15-30 Hz) pathological in PD.
citations: Nambu et al. 2002 (J Neurophysiol): Hyperdirect pathway timing
Miocinovic et al. 2018 (JAMA Neurol): STN DBS mechanisms
Walker et al. 2012 (PMC2852267): Short-latency visual input to STN via superior colliculus

regions.name: Substantia Nigra
regions.function: Dopamine production (SNc, pars compacta) and basal ganglia output (SNr, pars reticulata). Degeneration causes Parkinson's.

response_latency_ms.measure: Dopamine burst latency to unexpected reward
response_latency_ms.notes: SNc dopamine neurons: burst response to unexpected reward ~10-20ms. SNr inhibitory output timing similar to GPi.
citations: Schultz 1998 (J Neurophysiol): Dopamine neuron temporal dynamics

regions.name: Thalamus
regions.function: Central relay station for all sensory modalities (except olfaction). Gating via reticular thalamic nucleus (TRN) implements default-deny on ascending traffic.

response_latency_ms.measure: LGN relay latency (retina → LGN → V1)
response_latency_ms.notes: LGN visual relay: ~4.2ms (retinogeniculate delay). VPL somatosensory relay: ~14ms (median nerve). MGN auditory relay: ~6-8ms. TRN gating adds ~2-5ms.
citations: Sherman & Guillery 2006 (Exploring the Thalamus and Its Role in Cortical Function): Thalamic relay timing
Briggs & Usrey 2011 (Neuron): LGN response properties

regions.name: Hypothalamus
regions.function: Homeostatic regulation — temperature, hunger, thirst, circadian rhythm, hormone release, autonomic output

response_latency_ms.measure: Lateral hypothalamus evoked potential to sciatic nerve stimulation (cat)
response_latency_ms.notes: Lateral hypothalamus: ~7ms (cat, evoked potential). Acoustic/somatosensory: <15-20ms across mammals. Homeostatic outputs operate on seconds-to-hours timescale. CAUTION: 7ms value from animal data — human intracranial data extremely limited.
citations: Yamamoto et al. 2020 (Physiol Behav, DOI: 10.1016/j.physbeh.2020.113100): Fast sensory responses in lateral hypothalamus review

regions.name: Ventral Intermediate Nucleus (Thalamus)
regions.function: Cerebellar relay to motor cortex. Primary DBS target for essential tremor.

response_latency_ms.measure: Cerebellothalamic conduction time
response_latency_ms.notes: Dentate nucleus to VIM: ~5-8ms. VIM to motor cortex: ~3-5ms. Total cerebellar loop: ~20-40ms.
citations: Middleton & Strick 2000 (Brain Res Rev): Basal ganglia and cerebellar loops

regions.name: Anterior Nucleus of Thalamus
regions.function: Limbic relay, memory circuits (Papez circuit). DBS target for drug-resistant epilepsy.

response_latency_ms.measure: Hippocampal input relay time
response_latency_ms.notes: Mammillothalamic tract conduction to ANT: ~5-10ms. ANT to cingulate cortex: ~5-10ms.
citations: Fisher et al. 2010 (Epilepsia): SANTE trial, ANT DBS for epilepsy

regions.name: Cerebellar Cortex
regions.function: Motor coordination, error correction, timing, procedural learning. Purkinje cells are the sole output.

response_latency_ms.measure: Mossy fiber input to Purkinje cell response
response_latency_ms.notes: Climbing fiber input: ~10ms. Mossy fiber → granule cell → parallel fiber → Purkinje cell: ~15-25ms. Complex spike latency: ~10ms from IO input. Purkinje cells fire at 30-100 Hz tonic.
citations: Heck et al. 2013 (PubMed 23970855): Purkinje cell rebound timing
Johansson et al. 2014 (PNAS): Memory trace timing in Purkinje cells

regions.name: Deep Cerebellar Nuclei
regions.function: Cerebellar output nuclei (dentate, interposed, fastigial). Relay to thalamus and brainstem.

response_latency_ms.measure: Purkinje cell inhibition to nuclear rebound firing
response_latency_ms.notes: Rebound firing after Purkinje cell pause: ~15ms. Dentate nucleus to thalamus: ~5-8ms.
citations: Heck et al. 2013 (PubMed 23970855): Rebound activity in cerebellar nuclei

regions.name: Cerebellar Vermis
regions.function: Axial motor control, balance, gait, vestibular integration, emotional regulation

response_latency_ms.measure: Vestibular input to vermis response
response_latency_ms.notes: Vestibulocerebellar projections: ~10-20ms. Vermis involved in both motor and emotional processing (Schmahmann dysmetria of thought).
citations: Schmahmann 2019 (Cerebellum): Cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome

regions.name: Medulla Oblongata
regions.function: Vital autonomic centers — respiratory rhythm, cardiac center, blood pressure (vasomotor), vomiting reflex

response_latency_ms.measure: BAEP Wave II (cochlear nuclei in rostral medulla)
response_latency_ms.notes: Brainstem auditory evoked potential Wave II: 2-4ms post-stimulus. Cochlear nuclei generate Wave II. Medullary autonomic reflexes: 5-20ms.
citations: NCBI Bookshelf NBK597358: Brainstem auditory evoked response norms
Hall 2007 (New Handbook of Auditory Evoked Responses): BAEP wave generators

regions.name: Pons
regions.function: Relay between cortex and cerebellum, REM sleep regulation, auditory relay (superior olivary complex)

response_latency_ms.measure: BAEP Wave III (superior olivary complex in caudal pons)
response_latency_ms.notes: BAEP Wave III: 3-5ms. Superior olivary complex generates Wave III. Pontine nuclei relay cortical signals to cerebellum.
citations: NCBI Bookshelf NBK597358: BAEP wave generators
Wikipedia: Brainstem auditory evoked potential

regions.name: Midbrain (Mesencephalon)
regions.function: Visual/auditory reflexes (superior/inferior colliculi), dopaminergic pathways (VTA, SNc), pain modulation (PAG)

response_latency_ms.measure: BAEP Wave V (inferior colliculus vicinity)
response_latency_ms.notes: BAEP Wave V: 7-9ms. Inferior colliculus response. Superior colliculus visual saccade response: ~40-70ms.
citations: NCBI Bookshelf NBK597358: BAEP Wave V generator
Walker et al. 2012 (PMC2852267): Superior colliculus to STN short-latency visual input

regions.name: Reticular Formation
regions.function: Arousal, consciousness, sleep-wake transitions, pain modulation, postural tone

response_latency_ms.measure: Stimulus to arousal response
response_latency_ms.notes: Reticular activating system (RAS) response to salient stimuli: ~10-50ms. Startle reflex (reticulospinal): ~14ms. Muscle tone modulation: 10-20ms.
citations: Moruzzi & Magoun 1949 (EEG Clin Neurophysiol): Reticular activating system (classic)
Brown et al. 1991 (Exp Brain Res): Reticulospinal reflex latency

regions.name: Cervical Spinal Cord
regions.function: Upper limb motor innervation, diaphragm (C3-C5), neck muscles, upper limb sensation

response_latency_ms.measure: Corticospinal tract to cervical motor neuron
response_latency_ms.notes: Central motor conduction time to upper limbs: ~6-8ms (TMS). Peripheral conduction adds ~10-15ms.
citations: Rossini et al. 2015: TMS guidelines

regions.name: Thoracic Spinal Cord
regions.function: Trunk muscles, sympathetic outflow, intercostal muscles for respiration

response_latency_ms.measure: Corticospinal conduction to thoracic segments
response_latency_ms.notes: Longer conduction distance than cervical. Sympathetic preganglionic neurons here.

regions.name: Lumbar Spinal Cord
regions.function: Lower limb motor innervation, patellar reflex, lower limb sensation

response_latency_ms.measure: Corticospinal conduction to lumbar motor neurons
response_latency_ms.notes: Central motor conduction time to lower limbs: ~14-16ms (TMS). Patellar reflex arc: ~15-30ms total.
citations: Rossini et al. 2015: TMS guidelines

regions.name: Sacral Spinal Cord
regions.function: Bladder, bowel, sexual function, perineal sensation, parasympathetic outflow

response_latency_ms.measure: Corticospinal conduction to sacral segments
response_latency_ms.notes: Longest corticospinal conduction. Pudendal nerve latency: ~35-40ms total (central + peripheral).

regions.name: Cauda Equina
regions.function: Bundle of spinal nerve roots below L1-L2. Lower limb motor/sensory, bladder, bowel.

response_latency_ms.notes: Peripheral nerve bundle, not a CNS structure. Conduction velocity depends on individual nerve roots (~50-60 m/s).

regions.name: Central Amygdala
regions.function: Autonomic fear output, conditioned fear responses, pain modulation. Subcortical architecture (unlike BLA).

response_latency_ms.measure: BLA input to CeA autonomic output
response_latency_ms.notes: CeA receives processed input from BLA. Output drives autonomic responses (heart rate, freezing) with ~100-200ms latency from stimulus.
citations: LeDoux 2000 (Annu Rev Neurosci): Emotion circuits

device_mappings.notes: 1024-channel cortical implant in motor cortex. Read-only (recording spikes + LFP).
Next-gen: motor + visual cortex targets. Stimulation planned for vision restoration.
96-128 channels, penetrating electrodes. SIROF variant enables stimulation. Gold standard for cortical recording.
Uses Utah Arrays. Wireless variant: 48 Mbps, >36h battery. First home-use wireless intracortical BCI.
Responsive neurostimulation at seizure foci. Depth electrodes reach hippocampus, amygdala. Surface strips for cortical foci.
DBS + BrainSense LFP recording. Primary targets: STN (PD), GPi (dystonia), VIM (tremor).
Rechargeable variant. Same brain targets as Percept PC. >15 year service life.
Endovascular (superior sagittal sinus). Adjacent to motor cortex but inside blood vessel. No direct tissue contact.
Cochlear implant stimulates auditory nerve (CN VIII). Signal reaches A1 via brainstem auditory pathway. 22 electrodes.
Cochlear implant. 12 channels, longest electrode arrays (up to 34mm) for complete cochlear coverage.
DBS stimulation only (no sensing). 16-contact directional leads (Cartesia). 25-year rechargeable battery.
DBS with directional stimulation. Smallest non-rechargeable DBS device at launch. 8-contact segmented leads.
14-channel consumer EEG. Scalp recording. Volume conduction limits spatial resolution to ~cm scale. Saline felt sensors.
5-channel consumer EEG. Minimal coverage: frontal + temporal + parietal.
8-channel (expandable to 16) open-source EEG. ADS1299 24-bit ADC. Research-grade for non-invasive BCI.
4-channel entry-level open-source EEG. BLE. 18-bit effective resolution.
4-channel consumer EEG headband. Frontal + temporal positions. Dry electrodes. Primary use: meditation.
4 EEG + 2 auxiliary channels. Sleep tracking focus. Same electrode positions as Muse 2.
128-channel ECoG (subdural). Speech + hand motor areas. Temporary (6-month) implantation. Hopkins consortium.
421-1681 channels. Intracortical microwires (1.5mm depth). Near-infrared optical data link (not RF).
1024-channel thin-film cortical array (5um thick). Minimally invasive: inserted through <1mm slit. 510(k) cleared for intraoperative use.
8-channel research EEG. Hybrid dry/gel electrodes. 24-bit ADC. Bluetooth.
2000+ optical channels (TD-fNIRS). Hemodynamic imaging (not electrical). 52 modules. Whole-head coverage.
Research program (5 teams). Target: 16 channels in 16mm3 within 50ms. Approaches: acousto-optical, magnetoelectric nanotransducers, OPMs.

constraints.name: Thermal Power Ceiling
constraints.equation: P_total(n_ch, node_nm) <= P_thermal(R, n_chips, geometry, perfusion)
constraints.explanation: Total power dissipation must stay below the thermal limit set by brain region, chip count, implant geometry, and local blood perfusion. Exceeding this causes tissue damage.

constraints.name: Wireless Carrier Frequency
constraints.explanation: The wireless carrier frequency is limited by tissue attenuation at the implant depth. Higher frequencies lose more energy traveling through brain tissue.

constraints.name: On-Chip Clock Frequency
constraints.explanation: The on-chip clock speed is bounded by dynamic power dissipation (P ~ C * V^2 * f). Faster clocks burn more power, which feeds back into the thermal ceiling.

constraints.name: Moore's Law Scaling
constraints.explanation: BCI channel count doubles approximately every 7.4 years (Stevenson & Kording 2011). This governs when future attack techniques become feasible as hardware scales.

constraints.name: Shannon Electrode Safety
constraints.explanation: The Shannon safety limit constrains stimulation charge density (D) and charge per phase (Q). Exceeding k = 1.75 risks tissue damage from electrolysis and reactive oxygen species.

constraints.name: Signal Detectability (SNR)
constraints.equation: V_spike / V_noise_rms >> 1, where V_noise = sqrt(4kT * Re(Z) * df)
constraints.explanation: Neural spikes must exceed the Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise floor. At body temperature (310K) with 1 MOhm impedance and 10 kHz bandwidth, noise is ~13.1 uV rms.

constraints.name: QIF Coherence Threshold
constraints.explanation: The QIF signal coherence metric must stay above a minimum threshold for each brain function F. When Cs drops below Cs_min, the signal is either degraded, corrupted, or under attack.

constraints.name: Thermal Ceiling (Coupled)
constraints.explanation: Total temperature rise must stay below 1.0C (AAMI conservative guideline). This is coupled to constraint 1 via the Pennes bioheat equation. They are not independent.

constraints.name: Mechanical Mismatch
constraints.explanation: The ratio of implant stiffness to brain tissue stiffness must remain below a safe threshold. Silicon is ~6 orders of magnitude stiffer than brain tissue, causing micromotion damage.

constraints.name: Impedance Timeline
constraints.explanation: Electrode impedance rises over time due to gliosis (scar tissue formation). It must stay below the maximum for the target signal type, or recording quality degrades irreversibly.

constraints.name: Geometric Fit
constraints.explanation: The physical volume of the implant (determined by channel count and packaging) must fit within the target brain region. This limits maximum channel density per implant.

constraints.name: Information-Theoretic Minimum
constraints.explanation: The Shannon channel capacity must meet the minimum information rate required for the target function. This is a hard floor: no encoding scheme can beat it.

constraints.name: Wireless Telemetry Bandwidth
constraints.explanation: Total wireless data rate must accommodate all channels at their sampling rate and bit depth. This constrains how many channels can transmit simultaneously over the wireless link.

constants.source: Kim et al., Marblestone et al. 2013

corrections: Constraint 9 (mechanical mismatch): inverted ratio corrected to E_implant/E_brain < epsilon_safe
Johnson noise temperature corrected from 300K to 310K (body temperature): ~13.1 µV rms

BI.name: Biological Impact
BI.description: Severity of tissue damage from neural interface attacks

CR.name: Cognitive Reconnaissance
CR.description: Degree of unauthorized cognitive read access: thought decoding, neural data inference, intent extraction

CD.name: Cognitive Disruption
CD.description: Degree of unauthorized cognitive write access: perception manipulation, identity modification, cognitive coercion

CV.name: Consent Violation
CV.description: Whether the attack violates neural data consent

RV.name: Reversibility
RV.description: Whether neural or biological damage can be reversed

NP.name: Neuroplasticity
NP.description: Whether the attack causes lasting neural pathway changes

niss.formula: NISS = sum(w_i * M_i) / sum(w_i), default weights: BI=1.0, CR=0.5, CD=0.5, CV=1.0, RV=1.0, NP=1.0

tara.name: TARA — Therapeutic Applications & Risk Assessment
tara.description: Mechanism-first Rosetta Stone. Same physical phenomenon, four stakeholder views. Security researchers see attack vectors. Clinicians see therapeutic modalities. Regulators see compliance requirements. Engineers see physical parameters.

dual_use_classifications.confirmed: Published therapeutic and security applications exist for this mechanism
dual_use_classifications.probable: Strong theoretical basis for dual-use; therapeutic research underway
dual_use_classifications.possible: Mechanism could have therapeutic applications but no published evidence
dual_use_classifications.silicon_only: Pure digital/firmware/infrastructure attack with no neural therapeutic analog

consent_tiers.standard: Standard informed consent (data collection, non-invasive monitoring)
consent_tiers.enhanced: BCI-specific consent (stimulation, neural recording, neural data processing)
consent_tiers.IRB: Institutional Review Board approval required (research, novel therapeutic applications)
consent_tiers.prohibited: Application prohibited outside controlled research (unacceptable risk)

fda_statuses.cleared: FDA 510(k) clearance for predicate device equivalence
fda_statuses.approved: FDA PMA (Premarket Approval) for Class III devices

data_classifications.sensitive_neural: Neural data with enhanced protections (proposed neurorights)
data_classifications.PII: Personally identifiable information (GDPR Art. 9 special category)

dsm5.name: DSM-5-TR Diagnostic Mapping via Neural Impact Chain
dsm5.description: Maps BCI techniques to psychiatric diagnoses through the hourglass band model. NISS metrics predict diagnostic clusters. First known formal BCI threat-to-psychiatric-diagnosis taxonomy.
dsm5.positioning: RDoC-aligned, BCI-specific, with quantitative bridge (NISS) to traditional nosology (DSM-5-TR)

CR.risk_domain: Cognitive read access (thought decoding, neural data inference)

CD.risk_domain: Cognitive write access (perception manipulation, identity modification)

timeline.title: TARA v1.0: Initial Atlas Published
timeline.description: First public release of the Therapeutic Applications & Risk Assessment atlas. 60 attack techniques. The starting point.

timeline.title: ONI Framework Repository Created
timeline.description: First commit of the original ONI (Open Neural Interface) framework. Predecessor to QIF. 14-layer OSI model. Zero framework, zero scoring, just a question: what does security look like when the endpoint is a human brain?

timeline.title: ONI Demo Video v1.0 Complete
timeline.description: 3:56 animated explainer (Remotion + ElevenLabs). L1-L14 visualization, coherence gauge. First public-facing media.

timeline.title: Qinnovate Website Repository Created
timeline.description: First commit to the qinnovate.com website. Astro 5.x + React 19 + TailwindCSS 4. The public face of the project.

timeline.title: QIF Born: 14-Layer Model Deprecated, Hourglass Adopted
timeline.description: Single session produced 13 derivation insights. CNF renamed to QIF. 14-layer OSI model (v2.0) deprecated in favor of the 7-band symmetric (3-1-3) hourglass. QIF-TRUTH.md created as canonical source of truth.

timeline.title: First Cross-AI Validation (Gemini 2.5)
timeline.description: Independent AI peer review by Gemini 2.5 of the hourglass model. Validation pipeline established.

timeline.title: Hamiltonian Root: QI Grounded in Physics Formalism
timeline.description: The QI equation grounded in Hamiltonian formalism. Not a heuristic score but a quantity derived from the system's energy landscape. The unifying mathematical root.

timeline.title: L=v/f Unification and 11-Band Model
timeline.description: Wavelength equation applied to neural security. Government-restricted spectrum attack mapping added. QIF v4.0: 11 bands implemented (N7-N1, I0, S1-S3). Five coupling mechanisms identified.

timeline.title: NSP Formalized: 6-Layer Validation Stack
timeline.description: Neural Sensory Protocol designed as a 6-layer validation stack. Physics-based authentication using spectral invariants, microstate compliance, and challenge-response. Biological TLS.

timeline.title: NSP Goes Post-Quantum
timeline.description: Neural data has a decades-long sensitivity window. Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later threat model adopted. ML-KEM/ML-DSA integrated. 5 defense layers, Merkle-amortized SPHINCS+ signatures.

timeline.title: Black Hole Security Principle
timeline.description: Hawking/Susskind/Maldacena information principles applied to BCI security boundaries. Novel theoretical contribution.

timeline.title: Unified Neural Security Taxonomy
timeline.description: 60 attack techniques across 11 MITRE-compatible tactics spanning 7 domains. First structured BCI threat taxonomy.

timeline.title: NISS Created: BCI-Specific Severity Scoring
timeline.description: NISS (Neural Impact Scoring System) created because CVSS cannot account for biological reversibility, cognitive integrity impact, or neural tissue interaction. A severity metric built for brains, not servers.

timeline.title: First Multi-Model Validation Cycle
timeline.description: QwQ-32B, Grok-3, DeepSeek-R1, WhiteRabbitNeo, and Gemini independently reviewed QIF framework. Consensus reached on hourglass model validity. 5 errors corrected.

timeline.title: Project Runemate Conceived
timeline.description: Runemate DSL conceived: a domain-specific language for neural rendering. 3-pass Gemini review. NSP unification confirmed.

timeline.title: NISS v1.0 Finalized
timeline.description: Spectral decomposition bridge connects raw neural signals to per-band QI scoring. Every technique scored for neural impact. Replaces CVSS for BCI context.

timeline.title: Dynamical Systems Security: 6 New Attack Classes
timeline.description: Bifurcation manipulation, basin erosion, strange attractor injection, Lyapunov destabilization, phase space compression, and resonance entrainment. Novel BCI attack vectors from dynamical systems theory.

timeline.title: TARA Reframe: Threat Catalog Becomes Therapeutic Atlas
timeline.description: Attack techniques reframed as dual-use: every neural attack has a therapeutic analog. 35-40 therapeutic counterparts, 10 ambiguous, 18 silicon-only. Registry transformed from threat catalog to risk-benefit atlas.

timeline.title: Framework Complete (v1): QIF + NSP + NISS + TARA
timeline.description: First complete version of the full stack. 50 derivation log entries. 7 days of formal derivation. 4 framework versions. Ready for independent peer review.

timeline.title: TARA v1.3: S-Domain Consumer Sensor Expansion
timeline.description: Consumer device attack surface expanded. Synthetic band techniques for off-the-shelf sensors.

timeline.title: TARA v1.4: Consumer Side-Channel Techniques
timeline.description: 3 new techniques (T0100-T0102). Total: 102 techniques.

timeline.title: TARA-to-DSM-5-TR Diagnostic Mapping
timeline.description: Neural Impact Chain bridges attack techniques to DSM-5-TR diagnostic codes. 5 diagnostic clusters, 68 DSM codes mapped. Every TARA technique now maps to potential psychiatric outcomes.

timeline.title: Three Floors of Physics Integration
timeline.description: Landauer limit, Margolus-Levitin bound, and Heisenberg uncertainty integrated into QIF constraint system. P=E/t computational scaling equation derived.

timeline.title: NSP Core Implementation: Rust ML-KEM + ML-DSA
timeline.description: Post-quantum handshake implemented in Rust. ML-KEM-768 key exchange and ML-DSA-65 signatures working end-to-end. The cryptographic layer is real code, not just a spec.

timeline.title: Runemate Forge + NSP Integration Verified
timeline.description: End-to-end secure neural pipeline: HTML content compiled to Runemate Staves bytecode, encrypted via NSP post-quantum handshake, decrypted and rendered on target.

timeline.title: Zenodo Preprint v1.0 Published
timeline.description: First public preprint: 28 pages, 6 figures, CC-BY-4.0. DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18640106. 3 fabricated citations discovered and disclosed.

timeline.title: TARA v1.5: Neurorights Mapping
timeline.description: 4 neurorights (Mental Privacy, Cognitive Liberty, Mental Integrity, Psychological Continuity) mapped to all 102 techniques. Consent Complexity Index (CCI) introduced.

timeline.title: TARA v1.6: FDORA Regulatory Compliance
timeline.description: FDORA Section 3305 (Patch Act) coverage mapped to all techniques. Regulatory gap analysis complete.

timeline.title: BCI Hardware Inventory Published
timeline.description: 24 BCI devices catalogued from FDA filings, manufacturer datasheets, and peer-reviewed papers. Full specs with confidence levels.

timeline.title: Brain-BCI Atlas v1.2.0
timeline.description: 38 brain structures mapped to BCI devices, QIF bands, neural latency metrics, and physics constraints.

timeline.title: BCI Limits Equation Synthesized
timeline.description: 13 physics constraints unified into a single system coupling thermodynamics, EM, Moore's Law, Shannon safety, Boltzmann detectability, and QIF coherence. No published paper unifies all 13.

timeline.title: Physics Feasibility Tiering Complete
timeline.description: All 102 TARA techniques classified by physics feasibility tier. 61 feasible now, 11 near-term, 10 mid-term, 2 far-term, 18 no physics gate.

timeline.title: SSVEP Frequency Hijack (T0103)
timeline.description: Novel attack technique: hijacking steady-state visually evoked potentials via imperceptible screen flicker. Validated by Ming et al. (2023). 103rd technique added.

timeline.title: Zenodo Preprint v1.4 Published
timeline.description: NISS score corrections (arithmetic mean), TMS citation fix, dual-use label fix, Sherman & Guillery citation added. DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18677997

timeline.title: NSP Protocol Spec v0.5 Finalized
timeline.description: Protocol core and handshake complete. 5-layer defense stack, 3 device tiers, power budget validated at 3.25% of 40 mW.

timeline.title: Neurowall v0.7: Signal Integrity Monitor
timeline.description: 3-layer coherence monitor built from v0.1 to v0.7 in a single day. Multi-band EEG, spectral peak detection, CUSUM change detection, ROC analysis. 7/9 attacks detected at 15s, 9/9 at 30s. 14 attack generators in neurosim toolkit.

timeline.title: BrainFlow Real Hardware Validation
timeline.description: First test of Neurowall on actual EEG hardware (OpenBCI Cyton via BrainFlow). 5/5 attacks detected, 0% false positive rate. Coherence monitor works on real signals, not just synthetic.

timeline.title: BCI Limits Equation Cross-Validated (Gemini 2.5 Pro)
timeline.description: 12/13 constraints verified correct. 2 corrections applied: mechanical mismatch ratio inverted, Johnson noise temperature corrected to 310K.

timeline.title: Validation Dashboard + TARA Badges
timeline.description: Living validation system tracking cross-AI validation sessions, citation checks, and fact verification. Status badges added to every TARA technique page.

timeline.title: Repository Consolidation: 17 Directories to 8
timeline.description: Major repo restructure. Autodidactive removed, governance merged, archives cleaned. 11 READMEs and tables of contents written. Neurorights map added to root README.

timeline.title: Unified QIF API (/api/qif.json)
timeline.description: Single endpoint serving all QIF data: 103 threats, 24 devices, 38 brain regions, 13 physics constraints, scoring specs, timeline. CORS-enabled, open access. ~580 KB.

timeline.title: BCI Research Hub Launched
timeline.description: Dedicated /bci/ section consolidating hardware explorer, limits equation, security guardrails, and API documentation under one roof.

timeline.title: Automation Registry + Timeline Tracking
timeline.description: 30 automations catalogued (10 active workflows, 8 disabled, 6 scripts, 4 planned). Timeline staleness detection automated. Self-updating registry via daily GitHub Action.

timeline.title: TARA v1.7: Origin Classification + 6 New Techniques
timeline.description: All 109 techniques tagged with origin provenance (literature, qif_recontextualized, qif_chain_synthesis, qif_theoretical, neuroethics_formalized). Original authors credited. 6 new techniques added (T0104-T0109): 4 from Murcia/Lopez Bernal taxonomy gap, 1 neuromorphic mimicry, 1 data alignment exploitation. 24 new research sources added (165 to 193).

timeline.title: Neurowall v0.8: RunematePolicy Engine
timeline.description: L3 policy agent replaced 14-line if/else stub with a full rule-stack engine. 5 prioritized rules (critical_niss, high_niss, sustained_anomaly, growth_detected, spectral_peak) with cooldown, sustained window tracking, stimulation suppression, and configurable policy stacks. Gemini-validated (Phase 11).

timeline.title: CVE Disclosure Response Received
timeline.description: Coordinated vulnerability disclosure for BCI-adjacent streaming library received initial response. Public references genericized to protect disclosure timeline.

timeline.title: NISS v1.1: Cognitive Read/Write Split
timeline.description: Single Cognitive Integrity (CG) metric split into Cognitive Reconnaissance (CR, read-side) and Cognitive Disruption (CD, write-side). Weights normalized: CR=0.5, CD=0.5 preserving 20% cognitive share. Cross-AI validated (Gemini 2.5 Pro, Phase 16). 109 techniques rescored.

timeline.title: Zenodo Preprint v1.5 Published
timeline.description: NISS v1.1 CR/CD split, weight normalization, 6 metrics. DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18784513. All site documentation synced to v1.1.

timeline.title: FIRST.org CVSS SIG: NISS Accepted for Resources Repo
timeline.description: CVSS SIG Co-Chair Nick Leali confirmed NISS extension discussed by the SIG and offered inclusion in the official CVSS Resources repository (github.com/FIRSTdotorg/cvss-resources). First external standards body recognition of the framework.

timeline.title: Joined FIRST.org CVSS Special Interest Group
timeline.description: Registered as CVSS SIG member via FIRST portal. Contributing domain expertise on neural interface security scoring. The SIG noted no existing domain experts in BCI security among current members.
